title: The Principle of Inertia
description: Galileo argued that motion, once started, continues on its own unless something stops it — overturning Aristotle and pointing the way to Newton's first law.

title: Coordinate Geometry
description: Descartes joined algebra to geometry with the coordinate plane, giving physics the language to describe position and motion as equations.

title: Falling Bodies
description: Galileo showed that all objects fall with the same acceleration and that projectiles trace parabolas — the first quantitative laws of motion, built from careful experiment.

title: Calculus of Motion
description: Newton and Leibniz developed calculus, the mathematics of change that turns position into velocity and velocity into acceleration — the engine of all kinematics.

title: Newton's Laws
description: In the Principia, Newton stated three laws of motion that connect force, mass, and acceleration — the foundation of mechanics and much of physics ever since.

title: Hooke's Law
description: Hooke found that a spring's force is proportional to how far it is stretched — a simple linear law behind springs, elasticity, and oscillations.

title: Laws of Friction
description: Amontons found that friction is proportional to the load pressing surfaces together and independent of contact area — rules refined a century later by Coulomb.

title: The Science of Friction
description: Coulomb's prize-winning study distinguished static from kinetic friction and quantified both, giving engineers the friction coefficients still used today.

title: The Atwood Machine
description: Atwood's pulley apparatus slowed gravity's pull enough to measure acceleration precisely, offering the first clean experimental test of Newton's second law.

title: d'Alembert's Principle
description: d'Alembert recast Newton's laws so that motion problems could be solved with the methods of statics, a key step toward analytical mechanics.

title: Heliocentrism
description: Copernicus placed the Sun, not the Earth, at the center of the planetary system — a reordering that launched the Scientific Revolution.

title: Kepler's Laws
description: From Tycho Brahe's data, Kepler found that planets move in ellipses at varying speeds by precise rules — the empirical laws Newton would later explain.

title: The Moons of Jupiter
description: Galileo's telescope revealed four moons circling Jupiter — worlds orbiting something other than Earth, strong evidence for the Copernican system.

title: Centripetal Force
description: Huygens derived the force needed to keep a body moving in a circle, showing that circular motion requires a constant inward pull.

title: Universal Gravitation
description: Newton proposed that the same force pulling an apple down holds the Moon in orbit — a single law of gravity binding the heavens to the Earth.

title: Halley's Comet
description: Using Newton's gravitation, Halley predicted a comet's return decades in advance — a triumphant confirmation when it reappeared in 1758.

title: Weighing the Earth
description: With a delicate torsion balance, Cavendish measured the tiny gravitational pull between lead spheres, pinning down the strength of gravity and the mass of the Earth.

title: Vis Viva (Living Force)
description: Leibniz argued that a quantity proportional to mass times velocity squared is conserved in motion — the ancestor of kinetic energy.

title: Energy of Motion
description: Du Châtelet showed experimentally that a moving object's energy grows with the square of its speed, sharpening the concept that became kinetic energy.

title: The Word 'Energy'
description: Young introduced 'energy' as the term for a body's capacity to do work, giving physics one of its most central and unifying ideas a name.

title: Defining Work
description: Coriolis defined 'work' as force times distance, tying force to energy and giving mechanics its precise bookkeeping of energy transfer.

title: Heat Is Energy
description: Joule showed that a fixed amount of mechanical work always produces the same amount of heat, revealing heat as a form of energy.

title: Conservation of Energy
description: Helmholtz and Mayer established that energy is never created or destroyed, only transformed — the first law of thermodynamics and a pillar of all physics.

title: Quantity of Motion
description: Descartes proposed that the total 'quantity of motion' in the universe stays constant — an early, imperfect form of the conservation of momentum.

title: The Study of Impact
description: Marci made some of the first careful observations of colliding bodies, opening the study of how motion transfers in an impact.

title: Laws of Collision
description: Responding to a Royal Society challenge, Wallis and Wren worked out how momentum is conserved when bodies collide, distinguishing elastic from inelastic impacts.

title: Elastic Collisions
description: Huygens showed that in elastic collisions both momentum and vis viva (kinetic energy) are conserved, and that motion is relative to the observer.

title: Symmetry & Conservation
description: Noether proved that every conservation law springs from a symmetry — momentum from the uniformity of space, energy from that of time — a profound insight uniting the conservation laws.

title: The Pendulum's Rhythm
description: Galileo noticed that a pendulum's swing takes the same time whether wide or narrow, discovering the isochronism that makes pendulums natural timekeepers.

title: The Pendulum Clock
description: Huygens turned the pendulum into a precise clock and derived how its period depends on length and gravity, founding the physics of oscillations.

title: Torque & the Lever
description: Varignon expressed the turning effect of a force — torque — in terms of force and lever arm, systematizing the ancient law of the lever.

title: Rotational Dynamics
description: Euler formulated the equations of rotating bodies, introducing moment of inertia — the rotational counterpart of mass — and the laws of angular motion.

title: Couples & Angular Momentum
description: Poinsot analyzed pairs of forces (couples) and clarified how rotation and angular momentum work, completing the classical picture of rigid-body motion.

title: Foucault's Pendulum
description: Foucault hung a long pendulum whose swing plane slowly rotated, giving direct visible proof that the Earth turns beneath our feet.

title: Hydrostatic Pressure
description: Stevin showed that a fluid's pressure depends only on depth, not on the shape of its container — the hydrostatic paradox at the root of fluid statics.

title: The Barometer
description: Torricelli made the first barometer and produced a vacuum, showing that air has weight and presses on everything around us.

title: Pascal's Principle
description: Pascal showed that pressure applied to a confined fluid spreads undiminished throughout it — the principle behind hydraulic lifts and brakes.

title: Bernoulli's Principle
description: Bernoulli related a fluid's pressure to its speed, explaining why faster-moving fluids push less — the idea behind lift, venturis, and flight.

title: Equations of Fluid Flow
description: Euler wrote the equations governing an ideal fluid's motion, turning fluid dynamics into a mathematical science.

title: Turbulence & Flow
description: Reynolds identified when smooth flow gives way to turbulence, captured by a single number that predicts a fluid's behavior across scales.

title: The Mercury Thermometer
description: Fahrenheit's reliable mercury thermometer and reproducible scale made temperature a precise, shareable measurement for the first time.

title: Latent & Specific Heat
description: Black distinguished temperature from heat, discovering that melting and boiling absorb heat without a change in temperature.

title: The Carnot Cycle
description: Carnot found the ideal limit on how much work any heat engine can produce, planting the seed of the second law of thermodynamics.

title: Absolute Temperature
description: Kelvin defined an absolute temperature scale starting from the coldest possible temperature, giving thermodynamics a natural zero.

title: Entropy
description: Clausius defined entropy and stated the second law: heat flows one way and disorder tends to increase — an arrow of time for the physical world.

title: Kinetic Theory of Gases
description: Maxwell explained a gas's temperature and pressure as the statistics of countless colliding molecules, deriving the distribution of their speeds.

title: Statistical Mechanics
description: Boltzmann connected entropy to the number of microscopic arrangements of molecules, explaining the second law as overwhelming probability.

title: The Nature of Charge
description: Franklin proposed that electricity is a single fluid with positive and negative states, and his kite experiment showed lightning is electrical.

title: Coulomb's Law
description: Coulomb measured the force between charges and found it follows an inverse-square law, giving electricity its first quantitative foundation.

title: Animal Electricity
description: Galvani made frog legs twitch with a spark, revealing that electricity flows in living tissue and setting Volta on the path to the battery.

title: The Battery
description: Volta's stack of metal discs produced the first steady electric current, giving science a controllable source of electricity and creating the field of circuits.

title: Ohm's Law
description: Ohm found that current through a conductor is proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance — the basic relationship of every circuit.

title: Gauss's Law
description: Gauss related the electric field passing through a closed surface to the charge inside, a powerful tool and one of the four equations of electromagnetism.

title: Kirchhoff's Circuit Laws
description: Kirchhoff's rules for current at junctions and voltage around loops let physicists analyze any circuit, however complex.

title: Electromagnetism
description: Ørsted noticed a compass needle swing near a current-carrying wire, revealing that electricity produces magnetism and uniting two separate sciences.

title: Ampère's Law
description: Within weeks of Ørsted's news, Ampère worked out the mathematics of forces between currents, founding electrodynamics.

title: The Biot–Savart Law
description: Biot and Savart measured how the magnetic field around a wire depends on the current and distance, quantifying the field a current creates.

title: Electromagnetic Induction
description: Faraday found that a changing magnetic field creates an electric current — the principle behind generators, transformers, and nearly all electric power.

title: Self-Induction
description: Henry discovered self-induction — a circuit's tendency to oppose changes in its own current — the basis of the inductor.

title: Lenz's Law
description: Lenz found that an induced current always opposes the change that creates it — a direct expression of energy conservation in electromagnetism.

title: Maxwell's Equations
description: Maxwell unified electricity, magnetism, and light in four equations, and predicted that electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light.

title: The Law of Refraction
description: Snell found the precise rule for how light bends when passing between materials, the foundation of lenses and geometric optics.

title: The Spectrum of Light
description: Newton split white light into a rainbow with a prism and recombined it, showing that white light is a mixture of colors.

title: The Speed of Light
description: Timing Jupiter's moons, Rømer showed that light travels at a finite, measurable speed rather than arriving instantly.

title: The Wave Theory of Light
description: Huygens proposed that light spreads as waves, each point of a wavefront sending out new wavelets — a picture that would explain diffraction and interference.

title: Wave Interference
description: Young's double-slit experiment produced bright and dark fringes, showing that light interferes like a wave and settling a century-old debate.

title: The Wave Optics of Diffraction
description: Fresnel's mathematical wave theory explained diffraction so completely that it converted the skeptics, cementing light's wave nature.

title: The Michelson–Morley Experiment
description: Their precise experiment found no 'ether wind' affecting light's speed — a null result that would soon force Einstein's rethinking of space and time.

title: Radioactivity
description: Becquerel and the Curies discovered that some atoms spontaneously emit radiation, revealing that the atom is not stable or indivisible after all.

title: Quantum Theory
description: To explain the glow of hot objects, Planck proposed that energy comes in discrete packets called quanta — the reluctant birth of quantum physics.

title: The Photoelectric Effect
description: Einstein explained that light knocks electrons loose only in energy packets, showing light behaves as particles (photons) as well as waves.

title: E = mc²
description: Einstein showed that mass and energy are two forms of the same thing, related by E = mc² — the equation behind stars, reactors, and the atom's hidden power.

title: The Nucleus
description: Rutherford's foil experiment showed the atom is mostly empty space with a tiny, dense, positive nucleus at its center.

title: The Bohr Model
description: Bohr allowed electrons only certain orbits with fixed energies, explaining the sharp spectral lines of hydrogen and bringing quantum ideas into the atom.

title: Matter Waves
description: De Broglie proposed that electrons and all matter have a wavelength, blurring the line between particle and wave.

title: Quantum Mechanics
description: Schrödinger's wave equation and Heisenberg's uncertainty principle replaced fixed orbits with probability, our deepest theory of the very small.

title: The Neutron
description: Chadwick discovered the neutron, the neutral partner of the proton that completes the nucleus and explains isotopes.

title: Nuclear Fission
description: Meitner and Hahn found that a uranium nucleus can split in two, releasing enormous energy — the discovery that opened the nuclear age.
